title: When we survey the wondrous cross
firstLine: When we survey the wondrous cross
publicDomainBasis: Isaac Watts (1674–1748), Edward Miller (1735–1807). All public domain.

sections.label: Verse 1
sections.text:
When we survey the wondrous cross
On which the Lord of glory died,
Our richest gain we count but loss,
And pour contempt on all our pride.

sections.label: Verse 2
sections.text:
Our God forbid that we should boast,
Save in the death of Christ, our Lord;
All the vain things that charm us most,
We’d sacrifice them to His blood.

sections.label: Verse 3
sections.text:
There from His head, His hands, His feet,
Sorrow and love flowed mingled down;
Did e’er such love and sorrow meet,
Or thorns compose so rich a crown?

sections.label: Verse 4
sections.text:
His dying crimson, from His head
Spreads o’er His body on the tree;
To all the world then am I dead,
And all the world is dead to me.

sections.label: Verse 5
sections.text:
Were the whole realm of nature ours,
That were an offering far too small;
Love that transcends our highest pow’rs,
Demands our heart, our life, our all.
